rationale: Proposes an operationalizable layered framework for assurance and regulatory compliance in LLMs applied to code tasks.
title: Developing assurance cases for adversarial robustness and regulatory compliance in LLMs

rationale: Provides a strong governance-first execution framework for autonomous AI agents that is highly transferable to AI coding oversight.
title: TRACE: a governance-first execution framework providing architectural assurance for autonomous AI operations

rationale: Presents a methodologically robust protocol for optimizing monitoring thresholds and permission boundaries in AI governance, applicable to oversight contexts.
title: Computable design space exploration via governance frameworks: joint optimisation of permission boundaries, monitoring thresholds and user transparency policies (for proxy AI)

rationale: Focuses on evaluating AI-generated code specifically for digital forensics tasks rather than addressing human oversight or governance in a software engineering pipeline.
title: AutoDFBench 1.0: a benchmarking framework for digital forensic tool testing and generated code evaluation

rationale: Proposes an architectural control mechanism to constrain AI generation at the repository level, addressing the volume/quality problem in vibe coding, though lacking a direct human-oversight focus.
title: Contract-coding: towards repo-level generation via structured symbolic paradigm

rationale: Directly investigates and operationalizes dynamic developer oversight and autonomy calibration for AI coding agents based on empirical software engineer interactions.
title: Hedwig: dynamic autonomy for coding agents under local oversight

rationale: Offers theoretical frameworks on human participation and oversight in AI-mediated systems that can inform the structural design of AI code governance.
title: The geometry of human participation: the structural conditions of legitimacy in AI-mediated systems

rationale: Primarily a descriptive survey of generative AI applications for cybersecurity in the SDLC without an operationalizable framework for human oversight of AI-generated code.
title: Exploring the role of generative AI in enhancing cybersecurity in software development life cycle

rationale: Details broad AI regulatory and governance frameworks, providing a policy context transferable to the compliance and oversight of AI-generated code.
title: Passporting compliance for AI: operationalising governance via a clause-level crosswalk across EU, ISO/IEC, NIST, and CoE frameworks

rationale: Presents an agent-based framework for automated code review, which offers a transferable mechanism for LLM-as-judge oversight in software engineering pipelines.
title: Issue-oriented agent-based framework for automated review comment generation

rationale: Provides empirically backed theory on human-in-the-loop decision-making and automation bias that directly informs the limitations of human oversight for AI systems.
title: Why providing humans with interpretable algorithms may, counterintuitively, lead to lower decision-making performance

rationale: Analyzes real-world risks and governance needs for autonomous coding agents, highlighting the urgency of robust oversight mechanisms.
title: The infrastructure gap: why platform security cannot protect against agentic attacks

rationale: A systematic review highlighting the need for professional oversight and adaptive governance of generative AI within enterprise software architecture contexts.
title: Impact and implications of generative AI for enterprise architects in agile environments: a systematic literature review

rationale: Empirically investigates developer over-reliance on AI coding tools and the subsequent impact on security review practices and oversight workload.
title: My code is less secure with gen AI: surveying developers' perceptions of the impact of code generation tools on security

rationale: Proposes a machine-readable governance and auditability standard that could be transferred to the authorization and oversight of AI code generation pipelines.
title: AI deployment authorisation: a global standard for machine-readable governance of high-risk artificial intelligence

rationale: Introduces an operationalizable multi-agent adversarial verification framework that is highly transferable to automated oversight triggers for AI-generated code.
title: Verified adversarial reasoning intelligence (VARI): multi-agent adversarial verification for regulated AI decision-making

rationale: Analyzes the legal and regulatory definitions of human oversight in the EU AI Act, establishing the broader policy context for governing automated systems.
title: From 'human control' in international law to 'human oversight' in the new EU act on artificial intelligence

rationale: Focuses on a specific code clone refactoring tool rather than addressing the broader organizational practices or scalability of human oversight for AI-generated code.
title: Towards just-In-time, inclusive clone refactoring

rationale: Focuses purely on optimizing LLM prompt engineering for automated program repair using linter data, lacking any connection to human oversight or governance.
title: An empirical analysis of ESLint information components and their interactions in LLM-based program repair

rationale: Provides robust behavioral evidence on automation shirking and overreliance in human-in-the-loop systems, highly transferable to the pitfalls of manual AI code review.
title: Designing human-automation redundancy: overreliance, automation shirking, and the operator’s dilemma

rationale: Operationalizes a multi-agent framework for automated, auditable code review tailored to regulatory compliance, directly addressing the scalability of oversight.
title: Code prism: a multi-agent, multi-LLM, semantic indexing artifact for regulatory code audits — a design science research study

rationale: A descriptive survey of LLM capabilities for static vulnerability detection that does not address organizational governance or human oversight practices.
title: Large language models for vulnerability detection in static code analysis: a survey

rationale: Offers a comprehensive taxonomy of enterprise AI governance and authorization approaches, providing foundational terminology for structuring AI code oversight policies.
title: A taxonomy of AI governance approaches: distinguishing visibility, alignment, and authorization

rationale: This piece proposes formal verification for vibe coding, placed at the border due to a thin abstract requiring human review.
title: AI for software engineering: from probable to provable

rationale: Discusses legal liability and compliance for AI tools in a specific security context (penetration testing), offering some transferable insights for organizational AI policy.
title: Legal, policy, and compliance issues in using AI for security: using taiwan's cybersecurity management act and penetration testing as examples
